sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: Marcy Harman (buyer, Decision Maker)
SF Next_Step__c (undated): nurture intent, no quantified pain
metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem or ROI case has been articulated. On the 24 April 2026 intro call, Marcy Harman described her role ('I lead the strategic sourcing team at Options Clearing Corporation') and confirmed OCC does 'a lot of' RFPs/RFXs 'manually' via Excel and email, signaling a process inefficiency. However, she immediately stated 'I'm not actively in the market' and framed the call as exploratory ('I'm always keeping my eyes open for what different tools are out there'). No cost of manual process, cycle-time impact, error rate, headcount burden, or savings target was discussed. The Hackett Group benchmark (days to build an RFP reduced to 5–6 days) was presented by Zycus but not confirmed or quantified by the buyer. Without an active need, no quantified value case exists. The immediate move is to re-engage Marcy, validate whether the manual RFP pain has become acute, and quantify the cost (cycle time, FTE burden, error rate) if a real evaluation has started.

sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: Marcy Harman engaged as a polite learner, not an advocate; stated 'not actively in the market'
SF OpportunityContactRole: Marcy Harman sole buyer contact, marked Decision Maker but no champion behavior evidenced
62 days since last activity (25 April 2026) with no buyer-initiated follow-up
champion.narrative: No champion exists. Marcy Harman (Executive Director, Strategic Sourcing, Decision Maker contact role) is the only buyer contact engaged, and on the 24 April 2026 intro call she explicitly stated she is 'not actively in the market' and positioned the meeting as a learning session ('I'm always keeping my eyes open for what different tools are out there'). She engaged politely on Zycus functionality (asked about Oracle/Zip/CLM comparison, request portfolio management, Slack integration) but made no commitment, offered no internal advocacy, did not introduce other stakeholders, and did not suggest a next step beyond receiving information for future reference. The call ended with 'Bailey will send additional information regarding Zycus to Marcy Harman for future reference when OCC may need it' (action item, 24 April 2026) — a classic polite brush-off. Since then, 62 days have passed with no buyer engagement. Marcy is a polite, informed contact, not a champion. There is no evidence she has access to the economic buyer, no evidence she is advocating for Zycus internally, and no evidence of urgency. Champion-building is the most critical near-term move: re-engage Marcy, validate whether her need has escalated, offer a concrete value prop tied to her stated pain (manual RFP inefficiency), and request multi-threading to the Procurement Head (Annette Morales) and the CFO (Andy Naughton or Michael Nowak) to find a sponsor with budget authority and urgency.

sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: Marcy Harman named Oracle, Zip, CLM as comparison categories
Bailey Erazo (Zycus) stated 'we aren't necessarily an ERP system... we would essentially attach onto Oracle' and differentiated vs Zip (procure-to-pay only) and vs acquisition-based competitors
SF Competitors__c field: unknown (Q3 not pulled this sweep due to field availability)
competition.narrative: No active competitive evaluation is underway, but Marcy Harman named three benchmark categories on the 24 April 2026 call: Oracle (ERP/procurement module), Zip (procure-to-pay), and CLM tools (contract lifecycle management). She asked 'how you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like Oracle, Zip, CLM, and where do you see Zycus fitting' — signaling she is category-mapping Zycus against known players, not running a live bake-off. Oracle is the incumbent ERP at OCC (confirmed on the call), so Oracle Procurement/Sourcing is the implicit do-nothing/status-quo competitor. Zip and generic CLM tools (likely Ironclad, Icertis, or similar) are the mental benchmarks for procure-to-pay and contract management scope, respectively. No other vendor (Coupa, SAP Ariba, GEP, Jaggaer) was named. The competitive landscape is entirely category-positioning, not deal-specific competition, because no deal exists yet. When a real evaluation starts, the likely competitors are Oracle (incumbent inertia), Coupa or SAP Ariba (full S2P suite), and Zip or a standalone CLM (if OCC evaluates best-of-breed vs suite). The wedge is Zycus's single-codebase AI platform (vs Frankenstein acquisitions), 50% R&D investment (vs PE-driven feature churn), autonomous negotiation agents and Merlin AI (vs manual processes), and Oracle ERP integration depth (attach and extend, not replace). Until a live evaluation with named competitors begins, this is a positioning exercise, not a competitive fight.

sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: Marcy Harman stated 'we do a lot of' RFPs/RFXs 'manually' via Excel and email, but 'I'm not actively in the market'
identify_pain.narrative: One operational pain point was confirmed: OCC runs 'a lot of' RFPs/RFXs today, and the process is done 'manually' via Excel and back-and-forth emails (Marcy Harman, 24 April 2026 call). This is a real, stated inefficiency. However, Marcy immediately qualified it by saying 'I'm not actively in the market,' meaning the pain exists but is not acute enough to drive an active evaluation today. No other pain was articulated: no supplier risk exposure, no contract compliance gap, no invoicing/AP bottleneck, no procurement visibility issue, no audit/governance failure. The pain is acknowledged but not urgent, not quantified (no cycle time, FTE cost, error rate, or savings target), and not owned by a senior sponsor (no CFO, COO, or Procurement Head has validated it). The pain is real but dormant. The move is to re-engage Marcy and ask: 'Has the manual RFP process become a bigger burden since we last spoke? What would make solving it a priority this year?' and request she connect us to the pain owner (Procurement Head Annette Morales or the CFO) if the issue has escalated.

sources: No Avoma or Salesforce evidence of contracting, legal, InfoSec, or implementation discussion
paper_process.narrative: No paper process (contracting, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec, implementation scoping) has been discussed or discovered. The intro call covered Zycus functionality but no commercial or contracting mechanics. There is no MSA on file, no SOW template shared, no mention of OCC's procurement approval workflow, and no InfoSec or IT security review referenced despite OCC being a financial services clearing corporation (likely high security/compliance requirements). No systems integrator or implementation partner has been mentioned. Current_Contract_Expiration__c is unknown (Q2 field not returned this sweep). Until a real evaluation starts, the paper process is entirely unmapped. The move is to surface it proactively when re-engaging: 'When you do move forward, what does your contracting and legal review process look like, and does IT or InfoSec need to approve new SaaS platforms?'

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has been identified or engaged. The single contact, Marcy Harman (Executive Director, Strategic Sourcing), holds a Decision Maker contact role but on the 24 April 2026 call positioned herself as a practitioner evaluating tools for future reference, not as the budget authority. She asked 'how you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like Oracle, Zip, CLM' and engaged on functional fit (request portfolio management, Slack integration), signaling she is a technical evaluator, not the commercial sponsor. No CFO, procurement head, or C-level executive has appeared. The account-level contact roster includes Andy Naughton (CFO, Treasurer), Michael Nowak (CFO), Dan Busby (COO), and Annette Morales (Procurement Head), but none are on the opportunity contact roles and none have been mentioned in any activity. Until the economic buyer is mapped and engaged in a commercial or ROI discussion, this deal has no commercial anchor. The move is to ask Marcy directly: 'When you do move forward with a procurement platform evaluation, who controls the budget and approves the business case?' and request an introduction to that person or to the Procurement Head (Annette Morales).

sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: no process discussion, Marcy stated 'not actively in the market'
SF fields What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c: not queried (Q3 fields not pulled this sweep due to field availability)
decision_process.narrative: No decision process has been mapped. The single call was an individual learning session with Marcy Harman; no other stakeholders appeared, no evaluation timeline was discussed, no approval chain was mentioned, and Marcy explicitly stated she is 'not actively in the market.' There is no evidence of a buying committee, no mention of procurement governance (RFP process, legal review, InfoSec, contracting), no reference to the relevant stakeholder or IT involvement despite Oracle being the incumbent ERP, and no forcing function or compelling event. The deal is in Qualified stage with a 2027-04-13 close date (291 days out), but that date appears to be a placeholder with no basis in buyer-stated milestones. Until Marcy or another stakeholder articulates an evaluation timeline, the steps to approval, and who else must be involved (IT, Legal, Finance, InfoSec, the economic buyer), there is no process to map. The immediate move is to ask: 'When you do evaluate procurement platforms, what does your evaluation and approval process look like, and who needs to be involved?'

sources: Avoma intro call 24 April 2026: Marcy Harman questions on Oracle/Zip/CLM comparison, request portfolio management, Slack integration
decision_criteria.narrative: Marcy Harman named three evaluation dimensions on the 24 April 2026 call: comparison to the relevant stakeholder, comparison to Zip (procure-to-pay), and comparison to CLM tools, plus 'where do you see Zycus fitting' — signaling she is benchmarking Zycus's scope and positioning against category leaders. She also asked specifically about request portfolio management ('managing a portfolio of requests... renewals that are maybe 9 months in the future... I wanna keep an eye on them') and Slack integration, indicating workflow visibility and collaboration integration are decision criteria. However, this was an exploratory call with no formal RFP, no documented requirements, no weighted scorecard, and no stated evaluation process. The criteria surfaced are inferred from her questions, not confirmed as the formal decision framework. When a real evaluation begins, the formal criteria (RFP automation, CLM depth, ERP integration, AI capability, supplier network, portfolio visibility, Slack/Teams integration, cost) must be documented and weighted, and Zycus's differentiation (single codebase, 50% R&D, autonomous negotiation agents, Merlin AI) must be anchored to those criteria in writing.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS fields are null (not scored). On the 24 April 2026 intro call, Marcy Harman engaged on Zycus AI capabilities (AI RFP grading, autonomous negotiation agents, contract risk scanning, Slack/Teams AI help) and asked functional questions, signaling intellectual interest in AI-powered workflow automation, but she explicitly stated 'not actively in the market' and framed the call as learning for future reference, not an active AI-driven evaluation. No buyer-stated AI strategy, no AI requirements in an RFP, no mention of AI as a competitive differentiator OCC is seeking. Tier is AI Curious (open to learning about AI capabilities but not actively prioritizing AI in procurement decisions). If OCC launches a real evaluation, the AI story (Merlin negotiation agents, single-codebase AI vs bolt-on AI, 50% R&D investment in AI/innovation) must become the wedge against Oracle (not AI-native), Coupa (acquisition-based AI), and Zip (limited AI scope).

north_star_verdict.headline: Cold lead masquerading as opportunity. Single intro call 63 days ago where Marcy stated she is not in-market. Zero buyer engagement since. No champion, no pain, no timeline, no evaluation. This is a nurture contact, not a qualified deal.

best_practice_check.summary: This is an unqualified lead, not a qualified opportunity. Marcy Harman stated she is not in-market, no economic buyer or champion exists, no quantified pain or compelling event, and the deal has been cold for 62 days. The immediate priority is to re-engage with a value-led offer tied to her stated pain (manual RFP inefficiency), validate whether urgency has emerged, and multi-thread to power (Procurement Head, CFO) to find a sponsor with budget and timeline. If no urgency surfaces, recommend moving this to a nurture/marketing queue and removing it from the active pipeline.

competitive_position.summary: No active competitive evaluation. Marcy Harman named Oracle (incumbent ERP), Zip (procure-to-pay), and CLM tools as mental benchmarks on 24 April 2026 call, but no live bake-off or vendor shortlist exists. Oracle is the implicit do-nothing competitor (incumbent inertia). When a real evaluation starts, likely competitors are Oracle Procurement (status quo), Coupa or SAP Ariba (full S2P suite peers), and Zip or a standalone CLM (best-of-breed alternative). No current threat because no active evaluation. Wedge when evaluation begins: Zycus single-codebase AI platform (vs Frankenstein acquisitions), 50% R&D investment (vs PE churn), autonomous negotiation agents + Merlin AI (vs manual), Oracle ERP integration depth (attach and extend, not rip-and-replace).

competitors.name: Oracle ERP / Oracle Procurement
competitors.quote: We use Oracle... Oracle is an ERP system. We aren't necessarily an ERP system... we would essentially attach onto Oracle.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as Oracle's intelligent procurement layer: attach and extend Oracle ERP with AI-powered S2P (RFP automation, autonomous negotiation, Merlin AI, contract risk scanning) without ripping out the GL/HR core. Oracle Procurement is functional but manual and not AI-native; Zycus accelerates Oracle with modern workflow and intelligence.

competitors.name: Zip (procure-to-pay)
competitors.quote: How you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like... Zip. Zip is mostly a procure to pay... sending out purchase requisitions all the way to purchase orders, receiving invoices... more in our wheelhouse rather than a Zip.
competitors.how_we_win: Zip is procure-to-pay only (eProcurement, eInvoicing), not full source-to-pay. Zycus covers the full cycle: eSourcing (RFP/RFX/auctions with AI grading and autonomous negotiation), CLM (contract authoring, repository, risk scanning), supplier network, and procure-to-pay. If OCC evaluates Zip, position Zycus as the full S2P suite vs a partial P2P tool, with deeper sourcing and contract intelligence.

competitors.name: CLM tools (generic category)
competitors.quote: How you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like... CLM. For Forrester, that's all enticing source to pay... Gartner is just for contract management.
competitors.how_we_win: Standalone CLM tools (likely Ironclad, Icertis, Agiloft) handle contract authoring, repository, and workflow but lack upstream sourcing (RFP/RFX, supplier network, autonomous negotiation) and downstream P2P (requisitions, POs, invoicing). Zycus is a Forrester Wave Leader in CLM AND a full S2P suite, so contracts live in context of the sourcing event and the supplier relationship. Position as integrated S2P with best-in-class CLM, not CLM-only.

items.source: Avoma intro call: Marcy confirmed Oracle ERP in place; Zycus positioned as procurement layer that integrates with Oracle without replacing GL/HR modules

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Partial alignment on manual RFP pain and portfolio visibility need, but no active evaluation to validate fit. Marcy confirmed OCC runs many RFPs manually via Excel/email (pain point Zycus addresses) and asked about request portfolio management and Slack integration (both Zycus capabilities). However, she is not in-market, so expectations are exploratory, not deal-specific. No formal requirements, no weighted criteria, no documented fit/gap analysis.

hard.ais_why:
• Rep is keeping OCC's Marcy warm by inviting her to the Agentic AI Webinar — AI content is the chosen door-opener.
• OCC flagged a few gaps across their full source-to-pay stack after meeting at the Chicago Roundtable, but the conversation is still exploratory.
• Fresh post-event motion: opp created 4/9/26 with last activity 4/25/26, and the agentic AI webinar is the next planned touchpoint.
hard.next_step: Rickson : Bailey to send info and follow up. Keeping lead wam by sending invite for Agentic AI Webinar.

pulse.summary: Live: buyer call(s) read this sweep.

rep_outreach.note: rep reached out, awaiting buyer reply

deltas.subject: RE-ENGAGE the relevant stakeholder: 62 days cold since last activity (25 April 2026). Marcy Harman stated 'not actively in the market' on the intro call and no buyer-initiated follow-up has occurred. Before investing further sales effort, the deal owner must re-engage Marcy directly with a concrete, value-led offer: 'Has the manual RFP burden escalated since we last spoke? We've helped similar financial services firms cut RFP cycle time by 80% and reduce Maverick spend via autonomous negotiation agents. Worth a 15-minute ROI discussion with your team?' If no reply or continued disinterest, move this to marketing nurture and remove from active pipeline. A cold lead consuming forecast capacity is a misallocated resource.

deltas.to: MULTI-THREAD TO POWER: Single-threaded to a practitioner (Marcy Harman, Strategic Sourcing Director) who explicitly stated no active need. The account roster in
deltas.subject: MULTI-THREAD TO POWER: Single-threaded to a practitioner (Marcy Harman, Strategic Sourcing Director) who explicitly stated no active need. The account roster includes the Procurement Head (Annette Morales), two CFOs (Andy Naughton, Michael Nowak), COO (Dan Busby), and CIO (David Hoag) — none engaged on this opportunity. When re-engaging Marcy, request an introduction to the relevant stakeholder (Procurement Head) and the CFO to validate whether procurement platform modernization is a strategic priority for OCC and to map the budget authority. A deal without power-level sponsorship in a large enterprise (OCC is a significant financial clearing corp) will stall indefinitely at the practitioner level.

deltas.to: BUILD A CHAMPION OR FIND ONE: Marcy Harman is a polite, informed contact but not a champion (no advocacy, no urgency, no internal selling, no stakeholder introd
deltas.subject: BUILD A CHAMPION OR FIND ONE: Marcy Harman is a polite, informed contact but not a champion (no advocacy, no urgency, no internal selling, no stakeholder introductions). Champion-building must start immediately if this deal is to progress. On re-engagement, offer Marcy a concrete win she can own: a benchmarking analysis of OCC's RFP cycle time + Maverick spend vs peer financial services firms, a tailored ROI model quantifying the savings from automating her manual Excel/email RFP process, or an invite to a private roundtable with Zycus customers in financial services/clearing (peer validation). Give her ammunition to sell internally and a reason to advocate. If she remains passive, ask directly: 'Who at OCC would champion procurement modernization if it became a priority?' and request an intro to that person.

value.name: Oracle ERP / Oracle Procurement
value.quote: We use Oracle... Oracle is an ERP system. We aren't necessarily an ERP system... we would essentially attach onto Oracle.
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus as Oracle's intelligent procurement layer: attach and extend Oracle ERP with AI-powered S2P (RFP automation, autonomous negotiation, Merlin AI, contract risk scanning) without ripping out the GL/HR core. Oracle Procurement is functional but manual and not AI-native; Zycus accelerates Oracle with modern workflow and intelligence.

value.name: Zip (procure-to-pay)
value.quote: How you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like... Zip. Zip is mostly a procure to pay... sending out purchase requisitions all the way to purchase orders, receiving invoices... more in our wheelhouse rather than a Zip.
value.how_we_win: Zip is procure-to-pay only (eProcurement, eInvoicing), not full source-to-pay. Zycus covers the full cycle: eSourcing (RFP/RFX/auctions with AI grading and autonomous negotiation), CLM (contract authoring, repository, risk scanning), supplier network, and procure-to-pay. If OCC evaluates Zip, position Zycus as the full S2P suite vs a partial P2P tool, with deeper sourcing and contract intelligence.

value.name: CLM tools (generic category)
value.quote: How you see yourself comparing to other types of tools like... CLM. For Forrester, that's all enticing source to pay... Gartner is just for contract management.
value.how_we_win: Standalone CLM tools (likely Ironclad, Icertis, Agiloft) handle contract authoring, repository, and workflow but lack upstream sourcing (RFP/RFX, supplier network, autonomous negotiation) and downstream P2P (requisitions, POs, invoicing). Zycus is a Forrester Wave Leader in CLM AND a full S2P suite, so contracts live in context of the sourcing event and the supplier relationship. Position as integrated S2P with best-in-class CLM, not CLM-only.
